description: Fig. 1. Atomistic model of Schwarz crystal. (a) Atomic model of Schwarz crystal demonstrated by $2 \times 2 \times 2$ supercell, where pink and white represent coherent twin boundaries (CTBs) and grain boundaries (GBs), respectively (Left). It should be noted that to enhance the visualization of GB structures, bulk atoms are excluded. Curved GBs (Top right), i.e., minimal interface structure, and CTB network (Bottom right) are shown. (b) A cross-sectional view of the Schwarz crystal reveals that curved GBs are constrained by CTB networks through quadruple junctions, which are marked by red circles.

Fig. 2. The thermal stability of Schwarz crystals with or without (001) surfaces (a) Atomic structure of the Schwarz crystal models $( 2 \times 2 \times 2$ supercell) without (up) and with (low) surfaces, where blue, purple, red, and grey atoms represent face-centered cubic (FCC), body-centered cubic (BCC), hexagonal close-packed (HCP, identified as CTBs), and other atoms, respectively. (b) The evolution of atomic structures at 1000 K. (c) The potential energy as a function of time at $8 0 0 \mathrm { ~ K ~ }$ and 1000 K. (d) The fraction of atomic packing as a function of time at 1000 K. a b

Fig. 3. The stability mechanism of Schwarz crystal with (001) surfaces at $8 0 0 ~ \mathrm { K } .$ (a) The atomic structure $2 \times 2 \times 6$ supercell) with (001) surfaces after relaxation at $8 0 0 ~ \mathrm { K } ,$ , where blue, red, and grey atoms represent FCC, HCP, and other atoms, respectively. The quadruple junction lines (QLs) are marked by red circles. (b) The evolution of Schwarz crystal with (001) surfaces at $8 0 0 ~ \mathrm { K } .$ .

Fig. 4. The destabilization process of Schwarz crystal with (001) surfaces. (a) The Schwarz model $2 \times 2 \times 6$ supercell) with (001) surfaces, where blue, purple, red, and grey atoms represent FCC, BCC, HCP, and other atoms, respectively. (b) The potential energy as a function of time at $1 0 0 0 \mathrm { K } .$ . (c) The fraction of atomic packing as a function of time at $1 0 0 0 \mathrm { K } .$ (d) The evolution of atomic structure at 1000K, where the black dashed lines represent the top and bottom boundaries (i.e. interfaces between the Schwarz crystal and single crystal), and the black arrows indicate the directions of interface movement.

Fig. 5. The comparison of instability temperature for Schwarz crystals with different heights of (001) surfaces. (a) The evolution of atomic structure (the vertical height is equal to $3 5 0 \mathrm { ~ \AA } )$ ) with (001) surfaces at $1 0 0 0 \ \mathrm { K } ,$ , where red and grey atoms represent HCP and other atoms, respectively. (b) Relationship between surface energy $( \gamma )$ and current height $\mathrm { ( h _ { c } ) }$ of the model. The inset shows the schematic diagram of Schwarz crystal with different heights, where $\mathbf { h } _ { \mathrm { i } }$ and $\mathbf { h } _ { \mathbf { c } }$ represent initial and current heights, red and grey lines represent CTBs and curved GBs, respectively. (c) Left: Relationship between critical instability temperature $\left( T _ { c } \right)$ and surface energy. The blue hollow circles and red lines represent calculated and theoretical results, respectively. Right: The side views of surfaces for two representative models (i.e., $\scriptstyle \mathtt { h } _ { \mathtt { c } } = 3 4 6$ and $3 6 0 \mathring { \mathrm { A } } )$ , where red and grey atoms represent HCP and other atoms, respectively.

Fig. 6. The evolution of interfaces between the Schwarz crystal and single crystal at 1000 K for (001) surfaces. (a) The potential energy profile along vertical direction at different times. (b) The snapshot of potential energy distribution, where atoms are colored according to their potential energy. The high-energy and lowenergy regions are represented in red and blue, respectively. The grey arrows show the position of interfaces (i.e. top and bottom boundaries) versus time. The top and bottom boundaries of the Schwarz crystal gradually approach each other, indicating the transformation of Schwarz crystal to FCC crystal.

Fig. 7. The destabilization mechanism of Schwarz crystal with (001) surfaces. Up: Relationship curve between grains and QLs integrity during the high temperature (i.e. 1000 K) relaxation process of Schwarz crystal. Low: The evolution of atomic structure $2 \times 2 \times 6$ supercell) with (001) surfaces at $1 0 0 0 ~ \mathrm { K } ,$ , where red and grey atoms represent HCP and other atoms, respectively.

Fig. 8. The destabilization mechanism of QLs. Evolution of QLs decomposition, where red and grey atoms represent HCP and other atoms, respectively, and green lines denote the $1 / 6 { < } 1 1 2 { > }$ Shockley partial dislocations. The local enlarged diagrams are shown in the upper region.

Fig. 9. The band-shaped destabilization of Schwarz crystals with (111) and (110) surfaces. (a-b) The schematic diagrams of band-shaped destabilization for (111) and (110) surfaces, where blue, red, and grey lines represent surfaces, CTBs, and curved GBs, respectively. The QLs and regions of band-shaped destabilization are marked by yellow circles and dark grey rectangles, respectively. The distances between adjacent QLs are displayed, indicating that the most close-packed planes composed of QLs reside within the gray rectangles. (c-d) The evolution of atomic structure for (111) and (110) surfaces, where red and grey represent HCP and other atoms, respectively.
